title: The Cost-of-Production Number That Decides Whether Your Harvest Was Actually Profitable
linkedin:
A grain grower can tell you their yield to the tonne and their price to the dollar. Ask what it cost to produce that tonne, and the answer usually goes quiet.

Not because the number does not exist. It is scattered across seed invoices, fertiliser accounts, chemical spend and contract harvesting bills that nobody has ever added into one figure.

That gap matters most in a season like this one. Winter crops are already in the ground across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria. Most of the input spend is locked in. The only thing left to move before harvest is the price the market decides to pay.

A grower who does not know their true cost of production per tonne is effectively farming blind between now and the sale, hoping the number works out rather than knowing the exact price at which it definitely will.

The pattern we see across primary production businesses is not poor farming. It is a genuine profit number sitting one level below the one everyone tracks, invisible until cost is worked out per tonne or per head instead of per season.

Once that number exists, the question changes. Not "was this a good season", but "at what price does this season break even, and how far above that will it land."

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #ProfitabilityAdvice #FinancialStrategy
facebook:
Ever asked a farmer how their season went and heard the yield and the price down to the dollar, but nothing about what it actually cost to grow?

That is more common than you would think, and it is not because the numbers are hidden. They are just scattered across seed, fertiliser, chemical and contractor invoices that nobody has ever added up into one figure.

With winter crops already in the ground and most input spend locked in, the cost side of the ledger is basically set for the season. The only thing left to move is the sale price.

Anyone in agribusiness worked out their actual breakeven price per tonne this year, or is it still a rough feeling rather than a number?

#AustralianBusiness
google:
A note for primary producers across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria heading into the back half of the growing season: yield and price get all the attention, but the number that actually decides whether a season is profitable is cost of production per tonne or per head.

With winter crops already in the ground, most of this season's input spend on seed, fertiliser, chemical and contract work is locked in. The only variable left before harvest is the price the market pays. Producers who know their true breakeven number can weigh that price with confidence. Those who do not are effectively hoping the season works out rather than knowing the price at which it will.

The same gap shows up in cash flow. Input costs are paid months before harvest income arrives, so a profitable season on paper can still be a genuinely tight one for cash in the meantime. Mapping that timing, alongside the true cost per tonne or per head, gives a clearer picture than yield and price alone ever will.

Read the full piece on the blog.
